narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid LSAT score of 38—above the typical 36.7 average—your law school application stood out in some ways, but the admissions model ultimately predicted a 66.5% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, leading to the rejection. This prediction draws from patterns in data on thousands of past students, focusing on factors like grades, test scores, and background that best forecast bar success. The three biggest influences on your specific outcome were your LSAT, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2 (well below the average 5.53), and your Year 1 decile of 2 (compared to the typical 5.71). Your stronger-than-average LSAT was the top positive factor, nudging the model toward expecting you to pass by showing your aptitude for the kind of reasoning the bar demands. However, your lower Year 3 decile ranking had an even stronger pull in the opposite direction, signaling to the model that your later undergraduate performance placed you in the bottom ranks relative to peers, which often correlates with bar struggles. Similarly, your Year 1 decile contributed toward the failure prediction, as early low rankings like yours have historically been a red flag for long-term exam readiness. Other details, like your middle family income bracket and full-time student status—which is common for over 90% of applicants—played smaller roles and didn't sway things much. In short, while your LSAT gave you an edge over many applicants, the model weighed your consistently low decile rankings as bigger risks, pushing the overall outlook toward failure. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-income background; it's just how these academic signals stacked up against proven patterns. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, as even small improvements there could flip future predictions. You might also seek targeted LSAT-level prep for the bar or explore schools with holistic reviews beyond this model. With your LSAT strength as a foundation, targeted steps on grades could open doors soon.
